eugenesiegel: I think you might be right actually. I've just noticed that the first foundry block got stalled on its download
the "received block" must've been for a different block hash and I got them confused

(also related to investigating https://bnoc.xyz/t/two-block-reorg-at-height-941880/97/17) should we disconnect bip157 light clients that request blocks we don't know about or just ignore them? during that reorg looking through @achow101's logs it seems lots of nodes BIP157/BIP158 requests for the other side of the block race and got disconnected

technically this seems to be a bug in their implementation of bip157/bip158 (https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/blob/master/bip-0157.mediawiki#getcfilters) "StopHash MUST be known to belong to a block accepted by the receiving peer. This is the case if the peer had previously sent a headers or inv message with that block or any descendents. A node that receives getcfilters with an unknown StopHash SHOULD NOT respond."
which I'm guessing is anti-fingerprinting, but I wonder if it would be wiser to just ignore instead of disconnecting?
on looking more closely it appears those are one and the same node reconnecting  version "/btcwire:0.5.0/neutrino:0.12.0-beta/"

hi
#topic Net Split WG Update (cfields)

Finally some progress! I have a branch which cleans up all of the local address handling which is currently just a bunch of global functions used all over the place (GetLocal(), AddLocal(), etc). Ultimately they are all used for GetLocalAddrForPeer().

My first step has been just to remove the dependencies on CNode and move all of the functions into a new LocalAddressManager. For now, it's instantiated as a static global. Functionally there's no change but it's now 10x easier to understand how it all works and test. The next step will be to actually instantiate it and store it in node.
While working on it, I discovered a few nasty leaks that should potentially be fixed. I'm working on testing and documenting so that I can propose some fixes and behavioral changes.

Both streams (refactor and fixes) could potentially happen in parallel, but imo it's _much_ easier to understand what's going on and test after refactoring to a sane manager class. So I'll probably PR that work first.
Cool, anything else on net split?
Nope, that's it for now.
#topic QML GUI WG Update (johnny9dev)

The decoupling of qml from the qt widgets gui is done. A part of that also included the last piece of automated test tools for the project. Specifically gmock for mocking the wallet and node interfaces
hi
so the project now has comprehensive testing. unittests, qml tests, and end to end gui tests
Feature wise wallet import/restore was merged and a pr for wallet migration is up and this week i did all of the features for fee setting. Those will be PR'd the next couple of days

epicleafies has a bunch of PRs lingering for features he completed so my top priority is to finish reviews of all of those now that the test frameworks are settled and they all no longer have conflicts

#topic Libevent removal (pinheadmz, fjahr)

#32061 has been rebased following great reviews on code style and deeper http protocol. I am running fuzzers on the branch this week and will re-run my integration tests with lnd, electrs, etc as well. The first 7 commits add tests and utilities, and are split off in to two small PRs which are in review, and I'm pushing updates today: #34772 and #34905
https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/32061 | Replace libevent with our own HTTP and socket-handling implementation by pinheadmz · Pull Request #32061 · bitcoin/bitcoin · GitHub
https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/34772 | test: modernize interface_http and cover more libevent behavior by pinheadmz · Pull Request #34772 · bitcoin/bitcoin · GitHub
https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/34905 | Update string and net utils for future HTTP operations by pinheadmz · Pull Request #34905 · bitcoin/bitcoin · GitHub
From my side, I am still getting some good on the torcontrol PR (#34158) and respond to that as quickly as possible. I think it looks to be in pretty good shape by now.
https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/34158 | torcontrol: Remove libevent usage by fjahr · Pull Request #34158 · bitcoin/bitcoin · GitHub
*good review
That concludes the WGs unless I missed someone
#topic asmap file format & tooling (sipa)
Hi!
With the asmap data now built-in to the bitcoin core binary, i had a look at the tooling around it
and it's not entirely satisfying i think that the only actual encoder/decoder for the format is a largely untested (and probably barely reviewed) python script

we do have a well-tested interpreter inside bitcoin core that can lookup ASNs, but there is no end-to-end testing that the logic we use for creating the files (in python) actually matches what the interpreter reads
so what do people think about adding a bitcoin-asmap binary, with a c++ implementation of what is currently in the python script?
the code would be able to be covered by testing, but still only the interpreter would end up in bitcoind
sgtm
Probably depends where the rest of the tooling ends up?
sounds good to me
so this is just about the "ASN mapping to binary" part, and possibly the diffing/etc, it's not about the actual sourcing of the mapping, which is the kartograf project
FWIW, there seemed to be diverse opinions about what should go where so I planned to have that part of the discussion in person
(some discussion in #34842)
https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/34842 | contrib: add ASmap attestation scripts by jurraca · Pull Request #34842 · bitcoin/bitcoin · GitHub
sipa: Would that be something that you hack up in a few days or a bigger project?
Replacing the encoder/decoder doesn't change the status quo so should be uncontroversial in that regard?
There doesn't seem to be a good split on what should live where, or if asmap = core or not, and who's responsible for maintainig various things
(especially since this is all becomming (potentially) release blocking)
Murch[m]: Opus 4.6 converted the python code in one go to C++, produces bit-identical files, and is 10x faster... i'd obviously clean it up myself, but having something that already works is nice
with that, another thing i want to bring up... i've been experimenting with replacing the binary file format with some new insights and better compression
and have something that can realistically be 20% smaller, while still being interpretable on-the-fly, though the format does get more complex
does that sound like a trade-off that's worth looking into?
i expect it to be equally fast or faster
how much more complex? But 20% sounds great
fjahr: the interpreter is probably not that much worse, the encoder is pretty nontrivial
Isn’t it pretty small already?
sipa: I suppose the format changes could be done in python too? So they don't depend on figuring out the c++ tool first?
yeah, I was going to say the order of these is worth thinking about

cfields: i already have it working in c++... this was the actual motivation for me to start looking into it, because the new encoding scheme is significantly more computationally costly, so didn't want to keep doing that in python
Or to put it differently, how big is the file?
ok, makes sense
the current code i have is not nearly production ready... but it's good enough to give me an idea of how much smaller it can get
Murch[m]: around 1.6 MiB, and shipped inside our binaries
1.4 with filling
so we might cut down on some 250 KiB in binary size
So about 10% of our releasebinary, maybe more
Then I don’t think it makes sense to spend a lot of engineering on making it smaller unless there are other benefits
there is the possibility of shipped the data in a separate file that needs to be installed in some discoverable location, rather than inside the binary
i think there are convenience arguments against that, but if installer size is a concern, that's a much more significant improvement
Right, but anyone running it will immediately download hundreds of gigabytes of data and store at least several. In that context, does it matter much?
Maybe I’m missing something here
Murch[m]: math is fun
Yeah, sure. Just trying to understand the cost-benefit heer
Murch[m]: that's a good point yes - i feel our binaries are big, but looking at the blockchain size does put it all in perspective
s/heer/here/
well i think i'll start with c++ifying with the current format
based on the discussion here
Murch: valid point, but i am still very interested in seeing what we can get
Not trying to tell sipa what to do, but I’m not sure spending several weeks to optim
Murch[m]: too late
sipa: better testing is a very good argument for that
s/optim/shave a MB off of that file is the biggest bang for the buck ^^/
I'm not sure how relevant is the comparison between binary size and block chain size. But the point probably holds for our RAM usage defaults anyways.
it's 3 weeks worth of block headers worth of RAM that this may save :)
which is probably not relevant
Unless we start having one dbcache defaults per piece of hardware ever manufactured :p
hids
which in 2026 is $10k...
sipa: right
cfields: :D
Also, I think we’re getting a bit lost in details here
agreed
that's it for me, unless people want to discuss other asmap related things
sipa: presumably it's irrelevant for runtime ram once loaded though? Or is this representation persistent in memory?
cfields: the cool thing about this format is that it's interpretable without decompression
so it's literally just a blob built-in to the binary, and directly interpreted there
oh, neat.
Anything else to discuss?
hi

I also want to shill for input on this question from last week's irc meeting: https://bitcoin-irc.chaincode.com/bitcoin-core-dev/2026-03-19#1204579;
dzxzgthree: Error: That URL raised <HTTP Error 404: Not Found>
"As a -blocksonly node, should we immediately drop the compact block (since we did not send sendcmpct to our peer) or should we process the header if we receive the compact block?"
[bitcoin] maflcko opened pull request #34927:  test: Check that RPCs do not time out, even under load  (master...2603-test-rpc-sla) https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/34927
more discussion here: https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32606#discussion_r2886012883
darosior: hmm, good question
eh, dzxzgthree
Ok, I guess please follow up at the discussion linked
#endmeeting
fjahr: Meeting ended at 2026-03-26T16:38+0000
fjahr: Raw log: https://achow101.com/ircmeetings/2026/bitcoin-core-dev.2026-03-26_16_00.log.json
fjahr: Formatted log: https://achow101.com/ircmeetings/2026/bitcoin-core-dev.2026-03-26_16_00.log.html
fjahr: Minutes: https://achow101.com/ircmeetings/2026/bitcoin-core-dev.2026-03-26_16_00.html
dzxzgthree: also impressed with your 8 consecutive consonants

I would have to read about the tradeoff here. Obviously it seems to me that we should always (tm) process headers, but that also doesn't seem crazy to ignore broken peers that send cmpctblocks to blocksonly nodes.. 🤷
i don't know if it matters much
it feels wrong to try to deal with broken peer implementations, as it might encourage relying on that behavior
but also processing headers is so cheap...

Only orthogonally related, but we’ve seen more BIP submissions in the past couple years (probably because they get processed?), but especially lately, there have been a few that I think I’ve been the only reader of. If anyone is looking for some fun reading material, please feel free to review some BIPs.

sipa: darosior: right this is the exact thing that I'm stuck on, in general it seems advisable to ignore out-of-protocol behavior from peers, but I also feel like processing a header that you have is so cheap and so potentially valuable that it might be an exception
sipa: Processing headers early has some downsides. We have been looking at the recent 2-block reorg and there is a relay anomaly there
Murch[m]: i think if that's related, then that sounds like an unrelated issue
wait, that sentence makes no sense
that's unrelated I think, although notably I think that the 10-minute stalling delay has hit a lot of nodes: https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/33687 in the recent re-org
one of achow101's nodes and one of b10c's nodes were both stalled by what I'm pretty sure is the same strange peer

i mean: if header processing somehow contributed to the 2-block reorg issue, then we have an issue in our code that should be addressed independently of the question of whether to process headers in unrequested compact blocks
sipa: started writing that when fjahr asked whether there were other topics, but posted it a bit delayed.
It looks like there is a node blasting out header INV of every new block to loads of other nodes without sending the bodies.
Murch[m]: are there still 4/5 BIP editors?
Murch[m]: stalling peers on IRC with delayed messages?
As far as I can tell, there are currently 6, but the vast majority of activity has been from a single dev in the last three months
Murch[m]: these are bip130 headers announcements, not compact blocks, right?

fjahr: Yeah, it looks like several nodes we looked at got the header for the Foundry block pretty early, but then waited ten minutes because they didn’t get a high-bandwidth compact block announcement of the block
Yes, not compact blocks
sipa: the peer aggressively inv's new blocks, responds to our getheaders with a headers, and then stalls the getdata for the block
achow101 and dzxzgthree looked into it more yesterday
if there's ever a race and all of your HB peers happen to be on the same side of it as you you get stalled for 10 min
dzxzgthree: oh, so it's INV <-, GETHEADERS ->, HEADERS <-, GETDATA ->, nothing?
https://bnoc.xyz/t/two-block-reorg-at-height-941880/97/19 I wrote a timeline of it here
sipa: yes
dzxzgthree: so it's not even using sendheaders, interesting
why would that broken/attacking node reach many nodes with that strategy, when other nodes using direct headers and need a roundtrip less? is it connecting to everyone?

even once the HB peers reorg, they won't send the cmpctblock to you except for blocks with height greater than what they've announced per https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/master/src/net_processing.cpp#L2109, at least that was the conclusion from looking at this at coredev
If b10c still has the logs, it might be worth checking if the stalling node then and now have similar characteristics (same entity)?

lightlike: sipa: That's still a question, but it seems still to be relatively succesful, b10c I think will share their timeline table soon on bnoc but from glancing at it, this node also seems to have a lot of success being the first to announce HEADERS to one of b10c's nodes, although it is sometimes beat by direct headers annoucements

I don't want to share it on irc, but I posted the full log message on bnoc for the stall disconnect, which includes the ip address of this entity if anyone else wants to check their logs for similar strange behavior
they also have a unique user agent
I'll check a node i have handy here
What's the UA?
"semikek"
lol
(an outburst of audible laughter was heard in the chaincode office)
I grepped my historical debug logs for them. They were first seen by this node in Nov 2024
b10c's irc is broken and asked me to share: https://github.com/Charterino/bitlens-rs/blob/dad40cb2ed98d4ba99792bc0ee621ec2982b4b97/src/connect/mod.rs#L60
it is a bitcoin explorer project and it seems that maybe this is a bug;
Someone also showed a log of a node that stalled on Stacker News: https://stacker.news/items/1459181
still I wonder why it is so often faster to announce headers than other peers in the logs I've looked at so far
sipa, the better question is do we know of any nodes that actually send compactblocks to blocks only peers?

blocksonly is not the only instance under questioning in that PR, another situation is a node that fails to send a SENDCMPCT, and I think the question can just be put more broadly: Should headers of out-of-protocol CMPCTBLOCK messages be processed?
my first guess is that it really doesn't matter

with the question put broadly it's also easier to express the general risk: if nodes don't process the headers from out-of-protocol CMPCTBLOCK's, then any bug or vulnerability that causes a node to behave out-of-protocol with CMPCTBLOCK's becomes a much more dangerous DoS vector
b10c: wb
You got any new hot goss on the reorg?

catching up on meeting notes re the ASMAP discussion, would it not be better to use a seperate file for the binary ASMAP data instead of building it directly into the binary blob.  That way, other node implementations depending on the bitcoinkernel project could make use of the same ASMAP file.
that is, I think it preferable to provide a seperate bin for asmap data instead of building it into the release binary IIUC.
hmm maybe it doesnt matter, as long as theres a place to source what gets built into the release bin
yancy: All asmap files that are embedded in releases, as well as a lot more, are available at https://github.com/bitcoin-core/asmap-data. In addition there is #33920.
https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/33920 | Export embedded ASMap RPC by fjahr · Pull Request #33920 · bitcoin/bitcoin · GitHub
